Oh, which is something that can't die.

Like a plant that can't die. A flower that can't die. Immortal. But that's but for the flower.

This is for the flower in particular. It's not doesn't apply to humans this word. No. I mean I've definitely hum definitely used it in songs as a poetic resource but no.

Wow. Yeah. inc. But then there are very famous ones that don't exist in English like um messa messa you know so means above or over somero that's where it gets because it's above the head well we can fact check that.

Okay let's see it seems like it would be right could be maybe not. And then messa table, right? Yeah. So la so messa is the time spent around the table after a meal.

Oo, chatting, connecting, you know, and so messas can go freaking long in Latin America as an experience. Oh yeah. After eating, like we would finish lunch at my grandparents house on a Sunday at like 1:00 maybe and we would stay at the table until like 4:00 just chatting. Yeah, that's a good one.

I like that. It's a reason why it's not in English because as soon as you're done eating, next thing go. But that Yeah, that's that's what I mean by how learning a new language can help us reframe our entire perception of the world and how we do things. You know, spending time, right?

This is something that I find fascinating. Um, the verb to spend in English can be used for money or for time. So, you can spend 20 bucks on an ice latte, which you can't. this town you're getting.

No, you can't. Um, or you can spend time with your family and friends as it's as if it's the same kind of resource, you know, whereas in Spanish we have a verb for to spend money is seem and then you have the verb to pass, which is what we do with time. Right? It's not a resource that we can just like, you know, here you go.

Something that we have to pass through and often we don't get to choose, you know, how much of it we pass, right? It's not so tangible like, oh yeah, here you go. Yeah. Such a different experience that comes through in that.

Do you notice that there's two different versions of Val? There's like the the English version, the Spanish version as you experience the world. I often think about this when you're speaking. It's my rapid.

See, it's like very quick. Um because obviously practice, but for me, it comes in, I translate it, and then hopefully it comes out. Um but it seems like you have a a process that maybe you start thinking in English when you're talking in English. But does your experience change with these languages when you're using them?

It's a good question. I think that thinking about that means kind of like switching from one to the next. And the way the way that I see it, when you learn a new language, you add that on. You know what I mean?

Does that make sense? Like I had this to work with and now I have this to work with. So in my life, I'm pulling from both, you know, and I feel like when I have conversations, it happens all the time that I won't find the like I'll be talking in Spanish with a friend and I just can't find the concept in Spanish, you know, cuz whatever that concept is to me, it it integrated in English and so I need to go to English and vice versa happens all the time. Yeah.

the Spanglish that I hear here, even with my partner who's from Costa Rica and she's like constantly just switching back and forth, but it's automatic because she has access to both. Yeah. Just gives you so much more a repertoire. It's amazing.

And your surface lexicon expands. Yeah. Which I think the surface lexicon is so important. Like you're saying, those few hundred words we use, what's available to us to describe a moment, an experience, a situation, and the words that we unconsciously choose to use.

See, you know, and the way that our life can change if we change those words. That's how it felt like to me. Like before moving to Costa Rica, I felt like mentally I was in a very challenging place and I had a lot of very limiting beliefs. and then moving to Costa Rica and meeting people that were leading completely different lives to the one that I was leaving, you know, and the language that they used and the way I started incorporating subconsciously some of those words like manifestation.

manifestation meant absolutely nothing to me before I started hanging out with the crew, you know what I mean? And the moment manifestation made it into my lexicon, I started manifesting you know what I mean? And it's like, I don't know, it's like the words that we choose to use subconsciously every day in our lives have a lot of impact. I mean, that's what manifestation is all about, right?

But if you don't have the word like manifest in Argentina for reference means like um like a march or some sort of protest. So like it's like okay we can't go into the city today because this highway is closed because there's una manifest, you know. So clearly manifestation changed when I moved to Costa Rica like the meaning of the word. Wow.

Yeah. New possibilities. Yeah. Can we talk about the last pillar fun?

Yeah. Very curious with very important learning obviously a language with any new skill as well because to speak about surfing. I see you surf. I see you practice.

I see you go to hang 10 and fail. And I've seen you also do it every single time. I have not done it successfully. No.

Okay. I was going to say I saw it. Maybe I didn't. Maybe I saw five.

No. hanging tennis when all toes go to the front. Not the And working on it. Working on it.

Having fun though cuz I've seen you fail hard and then paddle back out just smiling. I was like, "Yes." Yeah. Like to fail, be bad at it, suck like we said. Yeah.

And it is an uncomfortable edge, I assume. But then you're also having fun. You're having fun in the discomfort. 100%.

Let's go into that. I mean, yeah, fun was the last pillar to add and it kind of just happened like the moment I thought, oh, consistency, consistency, because it doesn't happen overnight. Like, it takes a long time. Fun.

If you do, if you want to do anything for a long time, it has to be fun. You have to enjoy it. You know, I like when my students come to class and they're kind of nervous and they're like, "Oh," you know, and then as we get into it, they start to relax and unwind and they start talking about their days and making jokes, you know, and it's like that's where the learning actually happens. You got to feel safe and you got to feel contained in order to learn psychologically.

Can you talk about this a little bit? Why is play and fun important with learning a skill? Well, think about learning your mother language. Mh.

You know, it's like as kids, we look at the most important people in our lives, whoever that is, our parents, our grandparents, our teachers, and they're playing with us and they're making faces and we have our little books and we have our little toys and they're like, "Oh, El Pero the dog." I say, "Wow, wow." You know, or waffle, whatever it is. Wow. Woof woof. You know, and that and and we integrate it because we're making that connection, that neurological pathway has been built of like, oh, this is good.

This brings me joy, you know, or I learn from this, you know, and that's why it's that edge, you know. It's like when kids go and stick their fingers in the in the power thingy, you know, and it hurts and they're like, "Ah, I shouldn't do that again, you know. It's the same for us. We need to be in those uncomfortable situations and feel a little bit embarrassed." And honestly, just make peace with being a little bit embarrassed.

You know, it's like an it's an underexplored emotion. I truly believe it. There's so much good that comes from feeling a little bit embarrassed and testing the limits of what's possible and just yeah showing people that you're trying. You know, Spanish speaking people, they're not expecting you to have a perfect accent or to nail your thoughts like to the tea.

They just feel so grateful and I say this from my own experience. so grateful and so seen and so included when they see you trying to communicate with them in their own language. Trying to get to know them for who they truly are and not for the version of them themselves that they had to create in order to be able to communicate with you. Does that make sense?

It does. And I dance with that so often between trying to play with it because I know and research shows that in play we learn something 200% quicker. Yeah. Up to 200% quicker.

We can learn through playing with the process. And then I get hard on myself while I go and interact at a cafe or I see a friend that I know their native tongue is in Spanish and I'm like and then it gets tight and the pressure comes up like okay I'm more comfortable in English and I can you know speak much better in that space. So yeah it is a dance and I like that you brought up when we were kids because how rapidly we can learn. Do you bring that in with a lot of your students?

Like, how do you bring play and fun into the process? I know you do it with me, but I'm your special student. I know that. Yes.

No one compares to you. No, of course. I feel like for me um with my students it's always been and maybe this is like a little bit of a psychology background with my family but it's always been above anything else about safety first and them feeling held in the space that we share. You know, I always tell people like quote unquote, "My job would be so much easier if I just gave you 10 textbook exercises to work on while I sit there and look at my phone and drink my coffee." But I don't want to do that.

You know, vocabulary is very difficult to teach because we only have so much time and what am I going to do? Sit here and be like the kitchen laor, you know, like no So vocab is about what's relevant to you, to your life, right? So let's talk about you. Let's talk about your day.

If we're working on the future tense, let's talk about your plans for the next year, for the rainy season, for next week, which is what we did yesterday in our class. Exactly. You actually do this. True.

The workound for the future tense. Then if we're going to if we're learning about the past, let's talk about high school. Let's talk about college, let's talk about your childhood. But for a lot of people, this is a lot of trust that they have to build with me, you know, who am I stranger to them most of the time, you know.

So, first and foremost, we got to create that space, that rapport, that, you know, that trust. And it goes both ways. And it also requires me to open up sometimes in class, too. You know, it's like I'm not going to sit there and be like, "Tell me everything about your life." and then be like, I'm not going to tell you anything about mine.

You know, goes both ways. And that's how I end up making really good friends with all of my students and loving my job even more, you know? So, I see no downsides. Oh, it's great.

And I've talked about this before, but it's true that information and emotion together equals long-term memory. So, you're doing that beautifully. And I I remember most of our classes, it it evokes an experience. I'm not just thinking through things analytically.

I'm brought into it. I'm a very sematic person, but you use that and you're like, "Oh, let's then talk about that space that you're interested in." So then it evokes excitement and different things in me. So hopefully it goes in the category of of long-term memory, but that's it's so beautiful that you utilize that process. Yeah.

like when we were talking about like certain emotions and where they show up in the body. Great way to like learn vocab around those two things and sentence structuring. Yeah. Can we talk about skill transferring and what you think about that or feel about that because you learned English.

Yes. I heard a bit about your process with that. Don't know how you have an American accent. That's wild.

The fact that you've never been to the US, it's so impressive. U that's a whole other thing. Um surfing and being a musician. I'm sure there's many other things you're great at, but those three I know u from my experience uh very skilled at.

And so I wonder if somebody is learning something, whether it's a language or anything, do you see skill transfers across this? Maybe you can speak to that. Well, the most important thing that I learned about learning anything is that when you learn a new skill, and it could be as small as learning a new word in Spanish or a new structure, you know, there's a new synapsis, right? There's a new neuropathway that was created and that is basically a bridge.

M and then you can walk that bridge in multiple skills. So the important thing is just creating more and more bridges. You know what I mean? We only develop neurons if I'm not mistaken until we're 25.

Mhm. So then we only have as many as we got by that age, you know. And the process that we can do and this is brain health is creating more and more and more and more of those bridges and we do that by learning new skills and those bridges help us in multiple realms you know um that's why I always say that if you don't have any reason whatsoever to learn another language like no reason whatsoever you still have one reason reason and it's the same reason why you go to the gym even if you don't like it or why you go for a morning jog even if you don't like it and it's because you got to train the muscle and learning a language is like freaking I don't know man crossfit for the brain you know or or mapilates something you know it's and there's a lot of studies that have come out recently about um how learning a new language which reduces the risk of developing Alzheimer's or early dementia. Mhm.

You know, and it's because of those new bridges that we are creating. Is it ever too late to stop to start learning? What's the oldest client you've had? My that I've had or that I have currently either.

My oldest student right now, Tony, is 73. And I think my oldest that I had altogether was S and he was early 80s. It's never too late. And Tony has gotten fluent in four years.

Four years. Quatro. Fluido. And he still doesn't think it's enough.

And that's another thing you got to face. You know, you're on this boat and you just got to keep going, man. It's like surfing. Do you ever feel like, oh, I'm good enough of a surfer?

Yeah, I'm sure even Kelly is like, "Hey, could have landed that backside 360 air way better, dude." You know? Yeah. What would be your message for anybody coming to It could be Central America, Latin America. As somebody that's an English speaker, what would be your message to them?

Try try. Yes. just give it a shot, you know, and you're going to see the benefits in every angle, and it doesn't have, you may start I see a lot of people start, we've talked about this, I see a lot of people start learning Spanish from a place of guilt. You know, I feel guilty that I live in Central America, Spanish speaking country, and I don't speak the language.

That I've been here for 13 years and I don't speak it. So, I'm just going to go do class to ease consciousness. If that's why you start, perfect. Let that be why you're starting.

But I assure you that's not going to be why you stay in it. Why do you stay in it? Because you get to know yourself better. You get to know your community better.

You get to feel like you're a part of something that's really beautiful and that's so much bigger than you and you understand the real reason why you moved here in the first place. You know, it's like why why did we move to Costa Rica? You know, I lived in Argentina, had a beautiful family, had my apartment, had my career, had my thing. you know, why did I move to Costa Rica?

I didn't know. You know, I'm still trying to figure out, but clearly I was seeking something and somewhere in me or in me, I don't know. I knew that I was going to find it here. And I feel like Americans, English speakers have that feel that pull too and have that calling, too.

But the language barrier prevents us from truly accessing that. You know what I mean? And it doesn't mean that you have to become fluent. And it doesn't mean that you have to speak Spanish now, but just by trying and just by getting yourself into that space, you start opening doors, you know, and you get yourself closer and closer to the real reason why you're here.

And I feel like there there are consequences to not doing it and to people collectively in large groups not doing it because I see so many people come for a vacation and they get here and they're like, "Oh my god, nature so nice. Oh my god, not a 9 to5. So nice. Oh my god, we sit at the table after we eat.

So nice." And they're like, "Ah, I think I'm going to move here." And then they move here and they bring the freaking Hamptons with them, you know, and they build a house, a community of life that is essentially the same thing that they had there, you know, and I think that language is a great tool that we have to prevent that. Mhm. You know. Yeah.

As somebody who is building community here that is not from here, is there anything you feel like myself or people in the community of what I'm building, do you feel like there's anything that you feel like we're missing or could lean into more? O yeah, call me out, man. That's a big question. Yeah.

Honestly, I feel like just time spent with the people that are welcoming you into this place, you know? Mhm. Time spent with the kids playing ball in the street. Time spent at the library.

Time spent at the schools. Time spent at the local supermarkets. You know what I mean? Time spent in town.

You know, I feel like, and I say this, I don't say this only for English- speakaking people. I'm Argentinian and Argentinians have a reputation of only hanging out with other Argentinians. And you go to the beach and you see a group of 15 people, ah, the Argies, you know, they have their mate, they're doing their thing. That's true.

Yeah. And it can feel very tight sometimes. And I have a bunch of Argentinian friends and I love them, but I also make sure that that's not my only group. Mhm.

And I have Tikico friends and I have an American friends and I have European friends and I have you know what I mean? And I keep it diverse because that was the experience that I was seeking out. You know I want to connect with people beyond their nationality and where they were born. And I think that it's totally normal to want to stick with what's comfortable and what's familiar.

There's nothing wrong about that. I think a lot of people deal with a lot of shame and feel like a am I a bad person because I'm there's nothing wrong with sticking to what's familiar and what's comfortable. But I feel like also I feel obligated to call you out on that, you know, and be like, "Hey, you're missing out. Like you're missing out." Yeah.

One of the reasons I wanted you on today is because you're such a bridge person, which you just alluded to. you really are like you bridge so many different things and I like to think of myself as that and yet there's a lot that I can improve on just hearing you name those things like yeah I could definitely do that a lot more. How do you feel like you cultivate this piece of being a bridge person of really going out of your way out of the the comfort spaces? Why do you feel like you desire that and cultivate that?

Because especially in a town like this, I think we could use more bridge people on all sides, you know, everybody. I think the more we can bridge here and around the world, we're going to be in a better place. So, how does one start to cultivate that bridge personality? I feel like for me it kind of comes pretty naturally because I love to talk and because I'm a very social person.

Yeah. Um but I think that sometimes when we scared to talk to someone that has nothing to do with them and everything to do with us you know it's like the other day Trafiko was around I don't know if we are going to be able to keep this here but the other day Traffico was around and they were taking bikes so many bikes And I was so sad. I was with my friend Lou and we were both just like crying. So sad because all good with traffic, they got to do what they got to do.

But we were in in the road of my house, which is the Delmare road. There's a lot of construction around and most of the construction workers are people from Nicaragua, you know, and most of their bikes aren't legal. That's just what it is. It's very difficult to have a legal bike here and very expensive.

So they just have illegal bikes. Okay, sue them. And this old pen who had just finished working a 12-hour shift under the sun in hot Costa Rica all day for pennies probably was just getting out of work and in this dirt road, you know, it's not like we were on the main road on the dirt road. Traffico drove by and they stopped and they took his bike and me and Lou were just headed back towards Esparansa.

So we offered him a ride and he was going to walk from L section to Espiransa after a 12hour shift in construction and we drove him and I was like huh interesting. I think in any other context I mean as a girl sometimes I drive by constructions and I feel a little uncomfortable right and I think that's a very common experience. So I think in that context I would feel a little bit awkward about talking to these Nikarawan men, you know. Um but in the car and feeling so much empathy for what he had just experienced, we just got to talking and it was such an amazing conversation and I was laughing so hard.

And then at one point we asked him like, "Are you guys?" At that point we didn't know they were Nicaraguan. We could assume but we didn't know. So we asked them like are you from here and they it was two guys they looked at each other before replying and they hesitated you know because they could anticipate some sort of you know preconceived notion and then they said oh we were from Nicarawa and me and Lou were like oh my god we love Nicaragua we went surfing there boy is amazing blah blah blah blah and they were so happy and I was like man I don't put myself in situations like this often enough where I get to just talk to people and I feel like for a lot of Americans sometimes talking to tikos can feel like that you know feel like oh what am I going to have in common with this person what are they going to think of me like you know what I mean and it's so not about them it's about us so I feel like yeah getting to know yourself helps with that because the more confident you are in yourself the more secure you are in yourself, the easier it is to connect with other people because you're not worried about what they're going to think of you because you already know who you are. So, you can just focus on getting to know them.

Yes. All right. Final segment. Val's hot takes.

Jesus Christ. Let's uh can we sing this bit, please? Of course you can. I won't but takes I would love to know some of your hot takes around language around no anything else got hot takes um my hot takes around you know what my hot takes are you know what my hot take is for today let's stop ucing expat as a term to refer to people who moved from the states or Argentina or wherever to live in Central American countries.

We are immigrants. That's okay. It's not a bad word. There's nothing attached to the word immigrant.

Now the northern hemisphere in this continent has adopted a very negative view of what being an immigrant is. And like I told you, I think migration is our right as a species. All animals migrate. And what are borders?

It's a madeup concept that served us maybe for a while when traveling was something very difficult, right? And we were kind of like assigned a place, but that's gone. That's long gone. We need to move past it.

You know, you're in you're it's your right to chase the life that you want, whatever that is. And we are immigrants as well in this country, you know. So, expat. Okay, you heard it here.

That's the hot. We're trying to embellish something that doesn't need embellishment. Mhm. You know, it's beautifully said.

Yes. Is there anything that we didn't talk about that you want to make sure we touch on? Let me check on my notes. Yeah.

M did we we actually I think we touched everything. Touched it all. Wait, we've touched it all. Yes.

Then you know the final question. Oh boy. Okay. On the podcast.

Val. Oh no. In this moment without wait or pressure to have a right answer. What does it mean for you to be whole?

And we go on commercial break. We'll be right back. Um, well, I'm not going to lie, Ryan. I have been pondering on this question knowing that it was coming.

And I think it's very difficult to narrow down the answer to just one thing. I mean, the nature of the question is very expensive. And I am also 24 years old. So, I don't know what a whole human is yet, but I think that it for me a huge part of feeling whole in my life has to do with the balance of giving and receiving.

And I don't think that is a permanent state. I definitely think it's a temporary state and that the balance can tip, you know, either way, but the moments where I feel the most whole and the more the most at peace is when I feel like I've given and I've accepted because I struggle a lot sometimes with both, you know, and I think we all do. And I think it's very pretty to say, "Oh my god, I'm it's very difficult for me to receive. I'm so good at giving." I'm like, "If it's difficult for you to receive, it's probably difficult for you to give at times as well, you know, and that's that's something that I've experienced.

But when I feel like yeah, like I was able to show up for my community, for my family, for my friends, and I've also been vulnerable and open enough to accept and receive what they wanted to give me. I feel the best and I feel very whole, incredibly fantastic. Thank you so much for your presence today, your very first podcast. It was so beautiful.

Thank you for being here. Thank you for being my teacher. Thank you for being somebody who is so dear to me. I really appreciate you.

  • go on continuar / prosseguir
    “And we go on commercial break.”
  • figure out entender / desvendar
    “You know, I'm still trying to figure out, but clearly I was seeking something and somewhere in me or in me, I don't know.”
  • show up aparecer
    “like when we were talking about like certain emotions and where they show up in the body.”
  • end up acabar (fazendo)
    “And that's how I end up making really good friends with all of my students and loving my job even more, you know?”
  • feel like estar a fim / parecer
    “Do you ever feel like, oh, I'm good enough of a surfer?”
  • make sense fazer sentido
    “Didn't make sense, you know, and I kind of just gave up.”
  • kind of meio que / tipo
    “Didn't make sense, you know, and I kind of just gave up.”
  • sort of meio que
    “But if you don't have the word like manifest in Argentina for reference means like um like a march or some sort of protest.”
  • of course claro
    “Of course, we all love Elono.”
  • for sure com certeza
    “For sure.”
  • as well também
    “Very curious with very important learning obviously a language with any new skill as well because to speak about surfing.”
  • as soon as assim que
    “It's a reason why it's not in English because as soon as you're done eating, next thing go.”
  • you know sabe / entende
    “Didn't make sense, you know, and I kind of just gave up.”
  • i mean quer dizer
    “You know what I mean?”
  • i guess acho que
    “Oh my god, I'm going to have to ask you if they exist in English, I guess.”
  • right now agora mesmo
    “My oldest student right now, Tony, is 73.”
  • so far até agora
    “You've actually made it an enjoyable process so far and I am so grateful to you.”
  • used to costumava
    “Eventually, you just get used to it.”
  • have to ter que
    “Why does it have to be fun?”
  • want to querer
